section.title: Walk in Love and Truth
section.sectionRationale: From the greeting the elder turns to his first appeal. Overjoyed to find some of the children walking in the truth, he urges the old commandment to love one another, and then defines that love as walking in obedience to God. This is the positive heart of the letter, before the warning that follows.
section.thesis: The elder rejoices to see God's people walking in the truth, and he presses on them the commandment they have had from the beginning, to love one another, defining that love as walking according to God's commandments.
section.frameNote: Three beats: joy at children walking in truth; the old commandment to love; love defined as obedient walking.

verses.text: I was overjoyed to find some of your children {{peripateo|walking}} in the {{aletheia|truth}}, just as the Father has commanded us.
And now I urge you, dear lady, not as a new commandment to you, but one we have had from the beginning, that we {{agape|love}} one another.
And this is love, that we walk according to His {{entole|commandments}}. This is the very commandment you have heard from the beginning, that you must walk in love.

peripateo.gloss: To walk, and figuratively to conduct one's whole life. John loves this image: faith is a way of walking, a daily path rather than a mere feeling. To walk in the truth is to let the reality of God in Christ shape one's actual living, step by step.

aletheia.gloss: Truth, reality, what is genuine. Here it is something one walks in, a path lived out, not only a creed believed. The elder's joy is that the children are not merely holding right ideas but ordering their lives by the truth of God revealed in Christ.

agape.gloss: Love, self-giving devotion that seeks another's good. This is the commandment the elder presses, to love one another, and it is the same command God's people have had from the beginning. In this letter love is inseparable from truth and from obedience.

entole.gloss: Commandment, an authoritative charge. John uses it for God's directives, above all the command to love. Strikingly, love itself is defined by walking according to His commandments, so love and obedience are bound together and interpret one another.

basic.context: Having greeted his readers, the elder now makes his first appeal. He is thrilled to have found some of the lady's children living by the truth. On that happy note he urges the command they have always known: love one another. Then he explains what that love actually is, walking in obedience to God.
basic.hermeneutics: The elder deliberately calls the command to love both old and familiar, not a novelty. He also refuses to let love float free of obedience: love is defined as walking according to God's commandments, and the commandment is to walk in love. The two are locked together on purpose.
basic.originalLanguages: Walking, peripateo, is John's picture of the whole Christian life, a daily path. The children walk in the truth, aletheia, and are urged to love, agape, one another. And love is measured by walking according to His commandments, entole, so love and obedience define each other.
basic.typology: There is no formal type here, but the pattern reaches back and forward: the command from the beginning echoes the law of love God always intended for His people, and it is fulfilled and deepened in Jesus, who made loving one another the mark of His disciples.
basic.christ: The command to love one another is the very command Jesus gave His disciples, calling it new in the sense that His own self-giving love became its pattern. To walk in love as Jesus commanded is to walk after Him, and He is the one whose obedience and love were perfectly one.
basic.god: God is a Father who commands, and what He commands is love. He does not set truth and love against each other, nor love and obedience; He binds them together. To obey God and to love one another are, in His design, the same path.
basic.crossReferences: John 13 is where Jesus gives the new commandment to love one another. 1 John repeatedly ties love to obedience and to walking in the light. Together they show that real love keeps God's word.
basic.oneStory: God has always called His people to love, and here that ancient command is pressed again, now shaped by Christ. Love that keeps God's commandments and obedience that expresses love are one path, the way God's redeemed people are meant to walk until the end.

context.literary: The body of the letter opens with a note of joy, a common device in ancient letters, before moving to appeal. The three verses turn on repetition, walking, truth, commandment, love, and from the beginning, weaving a tight knot that binds love, truth, and obedience.
context.historical: The elder has met some of the lady's children, perhaps as they travelled, and found them faithful. In a setting where false teachers were pulling people away, the sight of believers walking in the truth was cause for real gladness and a fitting basis for his appeal.
context.canonical: The appeal restates the command of Jesus in John 13:34-35 and matches the argument of 1 John, where love is inseparable from keeping God's word. It stands in the long biblical line that makes love of God and neighbour the heart of obedience.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To express joy at the readers' faithfulness, to renew the familiar command to love one another, and to define that love as obedient walking, guarding it from becoming mere sentiment.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Verse 4 describes the elder's joy at what he found; verses 5 and 6 are plainly prescriptive, an urging that God's people love one another and walk in His commandments. The command binds every reader, not just the first.

debates.question: Why does the elder call the command both old and yet urge it so freshly?

views.label: Old in origin, ever fresh in practice
views.case: The command to love is not a new invention; it is what God's people have had from the beginning, going back to Jesus and beyond. Yet it must be pressed afresh in every generation because love is always in danger of cooling.

views.label: A guard against novelty-seeking teachers
views.case: By stressing that the command is old, the elder implicitly warns against those who peddle new teachings; the true way is the one heard from the beginning, not the latest innovation.

debates.bearing: Both readings hold: the call to love is ancient and authoritative, and its very familiarity is part of its safety, setting the faithful old path against the deceivers' novelties in the verses that follow.

study.originalLanguages: The passage turns on walking, peripateo, John's image for the whole shape of a life: the children walk in the truth, aletheia, and all are called to walk in love, agape. The elder then defines love with striking economy: love is that we walk according to His commandments, entole, and the commandment is that we walk in love. Love and obedience are not two things but one, each explaining the other, and both rooted in what God's people have had from the beginning.

typology.type: The commandment from the beginning to love one another
typology.fulfillment: The new commandment of Christ in John 13:34, where His own self-giving becomes the measure of the love God always intended among His people.
The life of discipleship following Jesus, who is the way, the truth, and the life, so that to walk in truth is to walk after Him.

study.christ: The command the elder renews is the very one Jesus gave in the upper room: love one another. Jesus called it new not because love was unheard of, but because His own laying down of His life became its pattern and power. To walk in love according to God's commandments is to walk after Christ, in whom love and obedience were perfectly joined. He kept His Father's commandments and abided in His love, and He calls His people onto the same path.
study.god: God is a Father who commands, and the substance of His command is love. He refuses to let love drift into sentiment untethered from obedience, or to let obedience harden into rule-keeping without love. In His design the two are one road. What pleases Him is a people who walk in the truth and love one another, and He has willed this from the beginning.

crossReferences.note: Jesus gives the new commandment to love one another, the very charge the elder renews here.
Love for God is defined as keeping His commandments, matching the definition of love in verse 6.
Christ keeps the Father's commandments and abides in His love, the pattern of love and obedience joined.
The command to love is both old, from the beginning, and new in Christ, exactly the balance the elder strikes.

study.oneStory: God has always called His people to love, and here that ancient command is pressed again, now shaped by the self-giving of Christ. In the Bible's one story, to walk in the truth and to love one another are not two paths but one, the way of a redeemed people. This positive charge is the heart the elder must protect, and it is why, in the next movement, he sounds the alarm against those who would lead the family off this road.

sermon.title: Response, Request, Reminder
sermon.why: Begg preaches this passage, 2 John 1:4-6, tracing the elder's joy over children walking in the truth, his request that they love one another, and the reminder that love means walking in obedience. A clear, orthodox treatment of this movement.

quiz.q: What made the elder overjoyed in verse 4?

options.text: He found some of the lady's children walking in the truth.
options.note: Verse 4 names his joy: children living by the truth, as the Father commanded.

options.text: The children had grown wealthy and successful.
options.note: His joy is over faithfulness to the truth, not worldly success.

options.text: The false teachers had finally been silenced.
options.note: That would be welcome, but the stated cause of joy is the children walking in truth, not the silencing of opponents.

options.text: The children had invented a new teaching.
options.note: The elder prizes the old command, not novelty; new teaching is the danger, not the joy.

quiz.q: How does the elder describe the commandment to love one another?

options.text: Not a new commandment, but one they have had from the beginning.
options.note: Verse 5 stresses the command is old and familiar, not an innovation.

options.text: A brand new rule he is introducing for the first time.
options.note: He expressly says it is not new but from the beginning.

options.text: An optional ideal for the especially mature.
options.note: It is a commandment for all, not an optional extra.

options.text: A hard saying that few could be expected to keep.
options.note: It is demanding, but the elder presents it as the familiar, binding path for all believers, not an impossible ideal.

options.text: That we walk according to God's commandments.
options.note: Verse 6 defines love as obedient walking, and the commandment as walking in love.

options.text: A warm feeling toward other people.
options.note: Love here is defined by obedient action, not mainly by feeling.

options.text: Tolerating whatever others choose to believe.
options.note: The letter ties love to truth and obedience, not to indifference about belief.

options.text: Doing kind acts whenever it is convenient.
options.note: Love does show in action, but verse 6 grounds it in walking according to God's commandments, not mere convenience.

quiz.q: How are love and obedience related in this passage?

options.text: They define each other: love is keeping God's commandments, and His commandment is to love.
options.note: The two are bound together, each interpreting the other.

options.text: Obedience matters, but love is a separate and higher thing.
options.note: Love is central, but the passage will not separate it from obedience; it defines love by it.

options.text: Love replaces obedience, so commandments no longer matter.
options.note: The opposite; love is defined as walking according to His commandments.

options.text: They have nothing to do with each other.
options.note: The passage locks them together deliberately.

questions: The elder was overjoyed to see believers walking in the truth. Whose faithfulness has recently brought you joy, and have you told them?
Love here is not a mere feeling but a way of walking, obeying God day by day. Where is God calling you to show love this week in concrete obedience rather than warm words alone?
The command to love one another is old, from the beginning, yet always needs renewing. In what relationship has your love cooled, and what would it look like to take up the old command afresh?
This letter refuses to split love from truth or love from obedience. Are you tempted to keep one without the others, and how might holding all three together change the way you treat people?

translationNotes.note: 'Some of your children' need not imply the rest were unfaithful; it likely reflects that the elder had met only some of them, and rejoiced at what he saw.
Where the BSB prints em dashes around 'not as a new commandment to you, but one we have had from the beginning', they are rendered here as commas per house style.
The Greek weaves love and commandment together tightly: love is defined as walking in the commandments, and the commandment is to walk in love, a deliberate circle binding the two.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 2 John 1:4-6. Em dashes in verse 5 rendered as commas per house style.
